mappings.rationale: Directly covers the rise of Islam, the life of Muhammad, and Islamic doctrine, all foundational to subsection B on the rise and spread of Islam.
Directly covers the full scope of this section, from the early caliphates through the Umayyad and Abbasid empires and their successor states.
The Koran is the foundational text of Islam; this section covers Muhammad's career and the rise of Islam including the doctrine of jihad and religious developments.
Directly relevant to subsection B.1.b on the life and career of Muhammad and the rise of Islam.
Relevant to the section's coverage of the geographic spread of Islam from Arabia across the Near East, North Africa, Spain, and Central Asia under various caliphates.
Relevant to subsection C.1.d on cultural life under the Abbasids, including theology, philosophy, and the influence of Greek and Persian thought.
Relevant to the section's discussion of the development of Islamic governance, the role of the caliph, and the fragmentation into religious sects.
Relevant to the section's coverage of the flourishing of arts and sciences under the Abbasid caliphate and the Samanid dynasty's patronage of learning.
Relevant to the section's treatment of Islam's emergence from the Abrahamic tradition and its interactions with Judaism and Christianity in the conquered territories.
The section opens with a discussion of the study of Islamic history, historical sources, and historiographic problems.
Relevant to the section's discussion of Islamic mysticism under the Abbasid caliphate and Sufi developments in the cultural life of Muslim states.
Relevant to the section's coverage of the Samanid dynasty's role in Central Asia, the Islamization of Turkic peoples, and east-west trade connections across the Islamic world.
